questions.question: In Angular 17+, why does @for require a 'track' expression?
options: For debugging purposes — the track value appears in browser DevTools
To give Angular a stable identity for each item so it can efficiently update only changed DOM nodes
To enable TypeScript type checking on the loop variable
To allow the loop to be paused and resumed asynchronously

questions.question: What is the difference between *ngIf and [hidden] for conditional display?
options: They are identical — both hide the element
*ngIf removes the element from the DOM; [hidden] keeps it in the DOM but sets display: none
[hidden] is deprecated — *ngIf is always preferred
*ngIf works with components; [hidden] only works with HTML elements

questions.question: How does [ngClass]="{'active': isActive, 'disabled': isDisabled}" work?
options: Adds both 'active' and 'disabled' classes regardless of conditions
Adds 'active' if isActive is truthy, 'disabled' if isDisabled is truthy — independently evaluated
Adds a single class string 'active disabled' based on the object
Replaces all existing classes with the ones specified in the object

questions.question: A custom directive uses @HostListener('click'). When does this event fire?
options: When any element in the application is clicked
When the host element (the element the directive is applied to) is clicked
When the directive's parent component is clicked
When a programmatic click() is called, not from user interaction

questions.question: In Angular 17+, the @if control flow has a significant advantage over *ngIf. What is it?
options: @if renders 50% faster due to Ivy compiler optimizations
@if provides type narrowing — TypeScript knows the type is non-null inside the @if block
@if supports async values without needing the async pipe
@if automatically cancels pending HTTP requests when the condition becomes false
